relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: The Lord sends seventy-two before his face into the places he will enter.
The harvest is abundant, and prayer asks for workers.
The mission goes as lambs among wolves.
They travel light and focused on the way.
Peace is the first word into a house.
Peace rests where peace can be received.
Hospitality becomes home and provision.
Receiving includes eating what is set before them.
Healing and nearness of the Divine reign belong together.
Rejected cities still receive witness.
Even dust is released, but the reign has drawn near.
Refused visitation carries consequence.
Woe names missed turning in cities that saw power.
Tyre and Sidon become witnesses against unresponsive familiarity.
Capernaum’s imagined height is brought down.
Hearing and rejecting the sent ones reaches the Sender.
The seventy-two return joyful over authority in the name.
Jesus sees the adversarial power falling like lightning.
Authority is given for protection and overcoming harm.
Joy is redirected from power to belonging in heaven.
Jesus rejoices in holy Spirit over revelation to infants.
Father and Son reveal one another.
The disciples’ eyes are blessed for what they see.
Prophets and kings longed for this seeing and hearing.
A lawyer tests him with the question of age-long life.
Jesus asks how he reads the Law.
Love of the Divine and neighbor becomes the command center.
Doing this is the path of life.
Self-justification asks where neighbor ends.
A wounded traveler lies between Jerusalem and Jericho.
A priest sees and passes by.
A Levite sees and passes by.
A Samaritan sees and is moved with compassion.
Compassion becomes wounds bound, oil, wine, animal, inn, and care.
Mercy continues at cost beyond the first moment.
The question shifts from who counts as neighbor to who becomes neighbor.
The answer is mercy practiced: go and do likewise.
Martha welcomes Jesus into her house.
Mary sits at the Lord’s feet hearing the word.
Martha’s service becomes distracted and troubled.
Jesus names her anxious many-things.
Mary has chosen the one necessary, beautiful portion.
